text: The surgeon begins by mobilizing the liver and lysing adhesions to explore the caudate lobe. Retro-hepatic dissection of the inferior vena cava is performed from the caudate lobe of the liver.

The surgeon applies the Pringle's maneuver to control hepatic inflow, followed by liver parenchymal transection. Intraoperative ultrasonography confirms the presence of a tumor thrombus in the inferior vena cava (IVC). Proximal and distal IVC clamps are applied, and a cavotomy is performed with partial resection of the IVC wall to remove the caudate lobe tumor. The IVC is repaired using a continuous running suture of proline.

The surgeon begins an aortocaval lymph node dissection from the retrocaval area, using a bipolar sealing device to perform a right adrenalectomy. Pathological examination confirms the presence of hepatocellular carcinoma, IVC tumor thrombus, and lymph node metastases.
